Analysis of the Global Transition from Fossil Fuels to Renewable and Nuclear Energy from 1990-2014¶

By Angela Liu

Introduction¶

Today, we are constantly reminded of the importance of reducing our reliance on fossil fuels and take up renewable energy to combat global warming. To summarize, renewable energy is energy collected from resources naturally replenished within a human lifetime. Common examples include wind, solar, and geothermal. Fossil fuels are a nonrenewable energy source that are used far more quickly than they are being replenished and burning them release greenhouse gasses that warm up the planet.

Despite hearing about frequent breakthroughs in renewable technology, I wonder how much progress have we actually made and when we can start relying on cleaner energy sources. It is only natural to ask when will we stop stop relying fossil fuels as our primary fuel source for energy? With this online tutorial, I hope to enlighten readers with the truth about energy production.

Gathering Data¶

The data I used come from a large csv file collect by the United Nations from Kaggle: https://www.kaggle.com/unitednations/international-energy-statistics along with an Energy Questionare Guideline to help explain the data.

I also copied data from https://www.eia.gov/international/rankings/world?pa=12&u=0&f=A&v=none&y=01%2F01%2F2018 into csv format.

Then I simply uploaded the csv file into my notebook and extracted it into a pandas Dataframe.

In [1]:
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import matplotlib as mpl
import seaborn as sns
import sklearn
In [2]:
df = pd.read_csv('all_energy_statistics.csv')
df.rename(columns = {'country_or_area':'country','commodity_transaction':'commodity',
                      'year':'year','unit':'unit','quantity':'quantity',
                    'quantity_footnotes':'footnotes','category':'category'}, inplace=True)

Tidying and Cleaning Data¶

That is far too many data and most of them are irrelavent to my topic. Stating with what's easy, I dropped a column I didn't need.

In [4]:

Even in unique categories, there is too much data here. Let's first isolate the important bits by only focusing on the top 30 energy producing countries.

In [16]:
# pads list with None until given length n
def pad(list, n):
    for i in range(n-len(list)):
        list.append(None)

       'wind_electricity'], dtype=object)

There are too many categories and we only want 7 of them: bio, geo, hydro, nuclear, solar, wave, and wind. However, according to the Energy Questionare Guidelines, most of these categories are variants of fossil fuels and biofuels. To group all these different categories together, I created a new Dataframe 'order', that lists keywords to determine which group each category belong to. I left 'electricity_net_installed_capacity_of_electric_power_plants', 'heat', and 'total_electricity' out because I decided these groups are too broad to be meaningful.

In [18]:
# list include all non-fossil fuel sources
# fossil fuel will encompass basically everything else so it's not listed
bio = ['fuelwood','charcoal','bagasse','animal_waste','municipal_wastes','industrial_waste','black_liquor','of_which_biodiesel','biodiesel', 'biogases','biogasoline','vegetal']
geo = ['geothermal']
hydro = ['hydro']
nuclear = ['nuclear_electricity','uranium']
solar = ['solar_electricity','thermal_electricity']
wave = ['tide_wave_and_ocean_electricity']
wind = ['wind_electricity']

pad(bio,len(bio))
pad(geo,len(bio))
pad(hydro,len(bio))
pad(nuclear,len(bio))
pad(solar,len(bio))
pad(wave,len(bio))
pad(wind,len(bio))
order = pd.DataFrame({'bio':bio,
                      'geo':geo,
                      'hydro':hydro,
                      'nuclear':nuclear,
                      'solar':solar,
                      'wave':wave,
                      'wind':wind})
order

In [22]:
def get_source(i):
    cat = df4['category'].iloc[i]
    sources = list(order.columns.values)
    if cat in sources:
        return cat
    for s in sources:
        if cat in list(order[s]):
            return s
    return 'fossil'

Now I will create a brand new dataframe that will only hold country, year, unit, quantity, source, and category. That way, it will be much easier to gather all data for a single type of energy source.

In [23]:
# takes a few minutes
df5 = pd.DataFrame(columns=['country','year','unit','quantity','source','category'])
for i in range(len(df4)):
    dict={'country':df4['country'].iloc[i],'year':df4['year'].iloc[i],'unit':df4['unit'].iloc[i],'quantity':df4['quantity'].iloc[i],'source':get_source(i),'category':df4['category'].iloc[i]}
    df5 = df5.append(dict, ignore_index=True)
In [24]:
df5.head()
Out[24]:
country year unit quantity source category
0 Colombia 2014 Metric tons, thousand 34.53 fossil additives_and_oxygenates
1 Colombia 2013 Metric tons, thousand 34.53 fossil additives_and_oxygenates
2 Colombia 2012 Metric tons, thousand 38.22 fossil additives_and_oxygenates
3 Colombia 2011 Metric tons, thousand 36.39 fossil additives_and_oxygenates
4 Colombia 2010 Metric tons, thousand 30.21 fossil additives_and_oxygenates

Now our data is in a much simpler form and read to be analyzed. Here are what the columns represent:

  • country: country where energy is produced
  • year: year of recorded production
  • unit: unit of measurement of the given quantity
  • quantity: how much of the energy is produced in given unit
  • source: one of the 7 groups of energy: bio, geo, hydro, nuclear, solar, wave, and wind
  • category: category of original energy data that gives more specific information about that energy

Exploratory Data Analysis¶

Now that we have our data in a nicer form, lets start by looking at energy production trends from 1990-2014 by the top 20 countries. To plot each energy source properly, we first need to make sure that all the data in each source have the same units.

In [25]:
for i in df5.source.unique():
    data = df5.loc[(df5['source'].str.match(i))]
    print(i)
    print(data.unit.unique())

fossil
['Metric tons,  thousand' 'Terajoules']
bio
['Terajoules' 'Metric tons,  thousand' 'Cubic metres, thousand']
geo
['Kilowatt-hours, million']
hydro
['Kilowatt-hours, million']
nuclear
['Kilowatt-hours, million' 'Metric Tons']
solar
['Kilowatt-hours, million']
wave
['Kilowatt-hours, million']
wind
['Kilowatt-hours, million']

From these lists, fossil, geo, and nuclear have multiple units within them. These units will need to be converted into Kilowatt-hours, million as that is what most of the other energy sources are recorded in. Here, I will assume 'Kilowatt-hours, million' means a million Kilowatt-hours(kwh), which is a Gigawatt-hour(gwh). Similarly, 'Metric tons, thousand' is a thousand Metric tons and 'Cubic metres, thousand' is a thousand Cubic metres.

In [26]:
df5.loc[((df5['source'].str.match('nuclear'))) & (df5['unit'].str.match('Metric Tons'))].category.unique()
Out[26]:
array(['uranium'], dtype=object)
In [27]:
# fixing nuclear is relatively easy as the only category that uses metric ton is uranium
# there are 45,000 MWh per tonne so 45 GWh per tonne
for i in df5.loc[((df5['source'].str.match('nuclear'))) & (df5['unit'].str.match('Metric Tons'))].index:
    df5.at[i,'quantity'] *= 45
    df5.at[i,'unit'] = 'Kilowatt-hours, million'
In [28]:
df5.loc[((df5['source'].str.match('bio'))) & (df5['unit'].str.match('Terajoules'))].category.unique()
Out[28]:
array(['animal_waste', 'biogases', 'black_liquor', 'industrial_waste',
       'municipal_wastes'], dtype=object)
In [29]:
df5.loc[((df5['source'].str.match('bio'))) & (df5['unit'].str.match('Metric tons,  thousand'))].category.unique()
Out[29]:
array(['bagasse', 'biodiesel', 'biogasoline', 'charcoal',
       'of_which_biodiesel'], dtype=object)
In [30]:
df5.loc[((df5['source'].str.match('bio'))) & (df5['unit'].str.match('Cubic metres, thousand'))].category.unique()
Out[30]:
array(['fuelwood'], dtype=object)
In [31]:
# fixing bio somewhat harder as there are now 3 diff units, each with different categories
# first convert m^3 to metric tons
# on avg 1 m^3 fuelwood/firewood is 1000kg = 1 metric ton = 1e-3 metric tons thousand
for i in df5.loc[((df5['source'].str.match('bio'))) & (df5['unit'].str.match('Cubic metres, thousand'))].index:
    df5.at[i,'quantity'] *= 1e-3
    df5.at[i,'unit'] = 'Metric tons,  thousand'
In [32]:
# convert metric tons to terajoules
# on avg 15 MJ/kg
# 1 k metric ton = 15,000,000MJ = 15TJ
for i in df5.loc[((df5['source'].str.match('bio'))) & (df5['unit'].str.match('Metric tons,  thousand'))].index:
    df5.at[i,'quantity'] *= 15
    df5.at[i,'unit'] = 'Terajoules'

df5.loc[((df5['source'].str.match('fossil'))) & (df5['unit'].str.match('Metric tons,  thousand'))].category.unique()

Here is the link to a helpful conversion table I found: https://unstats.un.org/unsd/energy/yearbook/conversion.htm

In [35]:
import re
# string of copy-pasted table
# might need to do some web scraping???
raw="""
Crude oil 44.53 42.3
Orimulsion 28.95 27.5
Natural Gas Liquids 46.53 44.2
Motor Gasoline 46.63 44.3
Aviation Gasoline 46.63 44.3
Jet Gasoline 46.63 44.3

Naphtha 46.84 44.5
Bitumen 42.32 40.2

Coal &
derived
substances

Other bituminous coal 27.16 25.8
Sub-bituminous coal 19.89 18.9
Lignite 12.53 11.9
Oil shale and tar sands 9.37 8.9

Patent fuel 21.79 20.7
Coke oven coke & lignite coke 29.68 28.2
Gas coke 29.68 28.2
Coal tar 29.47 28
Gas works gas 43.00 38.7
Coke oven gas 43.00 38.7
Blast furnace gas 2.74 2.47
Gaseous (kg/m3
)
a,b Liquid (kg/L)b Gaseous (lb/ft3
)
a,b Liquid (lb/gal)b
Range Typical Range Typical Range Typical Range Typical
Coal-based fuels
Anthracite coal
Oxygen steel furnace gas 7.84 7.06
Natural gas Natural Gas 53.33 48
Nonbiomass
waste fuels
Municipal wastes (non-biomass fraction) 10.53 10
Industrial wastes NA NA
Waste oils 42.32 40.2
Peat Peat* 10.27 9.76

fuels
Wood/Wood waste 16.42 15.6
Sulphite lyes (Black liquor) 12.42 11.8
Other primary solid biomass fuels 12.21 11.6
Charcoal 31.05 29.5
Biogasoline 28.42 27
Biodiesels 28.42 27

Landfill gas** 56.00 50.4
Sludge gas** 56.00 50.4
Other biogas 56.00 50.4
Municipal wastes (biomass fraction) 12.21 11.6"""

list1=re.split(" |\n",raw)
# first element is an empty string so get rid of it
list1=list1[1:]
list2=[]
# for each element, add to list2 if converting to float does not cause error
for l in list1:
    try:
        ele=float(l)
        list2.append(ele)
    except ValueError:
        pass
    
# print out avg of all float values
print(sum(list2)/len(list2))

33.2339423076923
In [36]:
# fossil fuels is the trickiest one. There are several categories in metric tons and each has
# a slightly different conversion to terajoules. Manually converting each one seems impractical.
# averaging all numerical values in pdf gives avg 33TJ/Gg
# 1 metric ton = .001 Gg
# 1000 metric ton = 1 Gg = 33TJ
for i in df5.loc[((df5['source'].str.match('fossil'))) & (df5['unit'].str.match('Metric tons,  thousand'))].index:
    df5.at[i,'quantity'] *= 33
    df5.at[i,'unit'] = 'Terajoules'
In [37]:
# now both bio and fossil are in terajoules, we can easily convert to gwh, or a million kwh
# 1 TJ = .2778 gwh
for i in df5.loc[(df5['unit'].str.match('Terajoules'))].index:
    df5.at[i,'quantity'] *= .2778
    df5.at[i,'unit'] = 'Kilowatt-hours, million'
In [38]:
# returns quantity for given country, year, and source
def get_quantity(country,year,source):
    total = -1   
    data = df5.loc[(df5['country'] == country) & (df5['year'] == year) & (df5['source'] == source)]
    if len(data) > 0:
        if total == -1:
            total = 0
        for d in data.index:
            total += df5.loc[d, 'quantity']

    return year, total
In [39]:
# default color cycle is too small so we make our own
mpl.rcParams['axes.prop_cycle'] = mpl.cycler(color=['#1f77b4', '#ff7f0e', '#2ca02c', '#d62728',
                                                    '#9467bd', '#8c564b', '#e377c2', '#7f7f7f',
                                                    '#bcbd22', '#17becf', '#ff7e7e', '#ffdb7e',
                                                    '#cdff7e', '#7effae', '#7effff', '#01009f',
                                                    '#5c009f', '#dddddd', '#ff7ec3', '#ff0000'])
In [40]:
num_rows = 4
num_cols = 2

fig, axes = plt.subplots(nrows=num_rows, ncols=num_cols)

sources = np.append(order.columns.values, ['fossil'])
i = 0
for r in range(0,num_rows):
    for c in range (0, num_cols):
        if i < 8:
            focus = sources[i]

            # plot line data for each country
            for C in countries:
                X=[]
                Y=[]
                for y in range(1990, 2015):
                    tup = get_quantity(C, y, focus)
                    # an element is only added to lists if that country listed that commodity that year
                    if int(tup[1]) != -1:
                        X.append(tup[0])
                        Y.append(tup[1])
                # plot for each country
                axes[r,c].plot(X, Y, label = C)
                
            axes[r,c].set_xlabel('Year')
            axes[r,c].set_ylabel('Total Production (Kilowatt-hours, million)')
            axes[r,c].set_title('Production of '+ str(focus) +' in 1990-2014')
            #wind's plot contains the most open space so that's where I show the legend(fix this)
            if focus == 'wind':
                axes[r,c].legend(loc='upper left')
            i += 1

# adjust size and padding
fig.set_figwidth(num_cols*8)
fig.set_figheight(num_rows*6)

plt.subplots_adjust(wspace=0.2, 
                    hspace=0.4)
In [41]:
num_rows = 4
num_cols = 2

fig, axes = plt.subplots(nrows=num_rows, ncols=num_cols)

sources = np.append(order.columns.values, ['fossil'])
i = 0
for r in range(0,num_rows):
    for c in range (0, num_cols):
        if i < 8:
            focus = sources[i]

            # plot line data for each country
            for C in countries:
                X=[]
                Y=[]
                for y in range(1990, 2015):
                    tup = get_quantity(C, y, focus)
                    # an element is only added to lists if that country listed that commodity that year
                    if int(tup[1]) != -1:
                        X.append(tup[0])
                        Y.append(tup[1])
                # plot for each country
                axes[r,c].plot(X, Y, label = C)
                
            axes[r,c].set_xlabel('Year')
            axes[r,c].set_ylabel('Total Production (Kilowatt-hours, million)')
            axes[r,c].set_title('Production of '+ str(focus) +' in 1990-2014')
            axes[r,c].set_ylim([0, 8e7])
  
            if focus == 'wind':
                axes[r,c].legend(loc='upper left')
            i += 1

# adjust size and padding
fig.set_figwidth(num_cols*8)
fig.set_figheight(num_rows*6)

plt.subplots_adjust(wspace=0.2, 
                    hspace=0.4)

This is not looking so good for renewable energy. If we look on the y-axis, fossil fuel productions are much higher that other sources of energy, more than 10 times in fact, and they still seem to be on an upward trend for most countries. In the second group of subplots, the only difference is I set the y-range to be the same for all line plots. This difference is so staggering that other sources of energy are barely visible above the x-axis.

Standardization¶

To plot linear regressions, we must see if the data is normally distributed. Using seaborn, we can use a violin plot to check that the distribution of productions was normal each year. Therfore I plotted a violin plot for each energy source with normalized quantities.

In [42]:
# normalize
df5['normalized_quantity']=np.where(df5['quantity']==0,0,(df5['quantity']-df5['quantity'].mean())/df5['quantity'].std())
In [43]:
df5.head()
Out[43]:
country year unit quantity source category normalized_quantity
0 Colombia 2014 Kilowatt-hours, million 316.550322 fossil additives_and_oxygenates -0.175620
1 Colombia 2013 Kilowatt-hours, million 316.550322 fossil additives_and_oxygenates -0.175620
2 Colombia 2012 Kilowatt-hours, million 350.378028 fossil additives_and_oxygenates -0.175574
3 Colombia 2011 Kilowatt-hours, million 333.601686 fossil additives_and_oxygenates -0.175597
4 Colombia 2010 Kilowatt-hours, million 276.947154 fossil additives_and_oxygenates -0.175673
In [44]:
for s in sources:
    d=df5.loc[df5['source']==s]
    plt.subplots(figsize = (15,8))
    sns.violinplot(x=d["year"], y=d["normalized_quantity"]).set(title=s)

Of all the violin plots, only two seemed reasonably normally distributed: geo and wave. All the others were right-skewed(skewed upwards) in varying amounts of severity. This makes sense because the top producing countries have the resources to produce much more energy when that energy is easily extracted. Geo and wave are both too location specific. Two out of eight is not a good percentage so perhaps we can sum up all the production in one year.

Regression¶

Now we will plot a regression line for all subplots to predict the trend in the future. Most of our original subplot do not form a linear relationship but perhaps summing the production of all countries each year will reveal a linear trend.

In [45]:
# will hold a dictionary of regression equation coefficients
equations={}
In [46]:
def get_total_quantity(year,source):
    total = -1   
    data = df5.loc[(df5['year'] == year) & (df5['source'] == source)]
    if len(data) > 0:
        if total == -1:
            total = 0
        for d in data.index:
            total += df5.loc[d, 'quantity']

    return year, total
In [47]:
num_rows = 4
num_cols = 2

fig, axes = plt.subplots(nrows=num_rows, ncols=num_cols)

i = 0
for r in range(0,num_rows):
    for c in range (0, num_cols):
        if i < 8:
            focus = sources[i]
            # plot line data for each source
            X=[]
            Y=[]
            for y in range(1990, 2015):
                tup = get_total_quantity(y, focus)
                # an element is only added to lists if that country listed that commodity that year
                if int(tup[1]) != -1:
                    X.append(tup[0])
                    Y.append(tup[1])
            # plot for each country
            axes[r,c].scatter(X, Y)

            m, b = np.polyfit(X, Y, 1)
            equations[focus]=(m,b)
            axes[r,c].plot(X, m*np.array(X) + b, color='orange',label='y={:.2f}x+{:.2f}'.format(m,b))
                    
            axes[r,c].set_xlabel('Year')
            axes[r,c].set_ylabel('Total Production (Kilowatt-hours, million)')
            axes[r,c].set_title('Total Production of '+ str(focus) +' of all countries in 1990-2014')
            axes[r,c].legend(fontsize=15)
            i += 1

# adjust size and padding
fig.set_figwidth(num_cols*8)
fig.set_figheight(num_rows*6)

plt.subplots_adjust(wspace=0.2, 
                    hspace=0.4)

Most of these plots do seem to have a linear trend now with the exception of wind. To address that, we could try a polynomial regression. However, which degree should we use?

Machine Learning¶

To tackle this problem, we can use supervised machine learning with holdout cross-validation to select the best degree. I set the training set to be 70% of the wind data and holdout set to other 30%. Then I iterate over a list of degrees ranging from 1 to 10. For each degree, I train features on the training set by tranforming existing features into a higher degree and fit them into Linear Regression. Then I predict on that training set and evaluate how well my prediction performed under the holdout set by extracting the rMSE (square root of the mean squared error). The degree with the lowest rMSE is the best fitted one.

As degree increases, our model becomes more complex and training loss will always decrease. However, generalization loss only decreases up to a point and then start to increase because of overfitting. Not only that, complex models require extremely large coefficients on polynomials. Therefore we want the minimum degree that gives the best performance.

In [48]:
x=[get_total_quantity(i,'wind')[0] for i in range(1990,2015)]
y=[get_total_quantity(i,'wind')[1] for i in range(1990,2015)]
In [49]:
from sklearn.linear_model import LinearRegression
from sklearn.preprocessing import PolynomialFeatures
from sklearn.metrics import mean_squared_error
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split

X = np.array(x).reshape(-1, 1)
y = y

x_train, x_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(X, y, test_size=0.3)

rmses = []
degrees = np.arange(1, 10)
min_rmse, min_deg = 1e10, 0

for deg in degrees:

    # Train features
    poly_features = PolynomialFeatures(degree=deg, include_bias=False)
    x_poly_train = poly_features.fit_transform(x_train)

    # Linear regression
    poly_reg = LinearRegression()
    poly_reg.fit(x_poly_train, y_train)

    # Compare with test data
    x_poly_test = poly_features.fit_transform(x_test)
    poly_predict = poly_reg.predict(x_poly_test)
    poly_mse = mean_squared_error(y_test, poly_predict)
    poly_rmse = np.sqrt(poly_mse)
    rmses.append(poly_rmse)
    
    # Cross-validation of degree
    if min_rmse > poly_rmse:
        min_rmse = poly_rmse
        min_deg = deg

# Plot and present results
print('Best degree {} with RMSE {}'.format(min_deg, min_rmse))
        
fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.plot(degrees, rmses)
ax.set_yscale('log')
ax.set_xlabel('Degree')
ax.set_ylabel('RMSE')
fig.set_figwidth(13)
fig.set_figheight(8)

Best degree 9 with RMSE 9641.802494965594

From the plot showing the rMSE's of each degree, we can clearly see dramatic improvement from linear to quadratic and then to cubic regressions. However, any higher and we receive diminishing returns. 9 may have the lowerst rMSE(expected since it is the max degree we tested) but 3 seems the most practical. Therefore we choose cubic.

In [50]:
# define the true objective function
def objective(x, a, b, c, d):
    return (a * x) + (b * x**2) + (c * x**3) + d
In [51]:
from scipy.optimize import curve_fit
from matplotlib import pyplot
from numpy import arange
popt, _ = curve_fit(objective, x, y)

# get coefficients
a, b, c, d = popt
equations['wind']=(a,b,c,d)
# plot input vs output
fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.scatter(x, y)
# define a sequence of inputs between the smallest and largest known inputs
x_line = arange(min(x), max(x), 1)
# calculate the output for the range
y_line = objective(x_line, a, b, c, d)

# create a line plot for the mapping function
ax.plot(x_line, y_line, '-', color='red',label='y={:.2f}x+{:.2f}x^2+{:.2f}x^3+{:.2f}'.format(a,b,c,d))
ax.set_title('Total Production of Wind from 1990-2014')
ax.set_xlabel('Year')
ax.set_ylabel('Total Production (Kilowatt-hours, million)')
ax.legend(fontsize=15)
fig.set_figwidth(13)
fig.set_figheight(8)

Using scipy's curve_fit function, we found a cubic function that fits our wind data much more nicely than a linear one.

Hypothesis testing¶

We have our regression equations and they seem to fit very nicely but to make sure of this, we need to do some hypothesis testing. When performing hypothesis testing on a regression line, our null hypothesis will always be all the slope parameters ($\beta _i$) are equal to 0. In other words, will the regression model contain at least one useful predictor? If our p-value is less than our significance level $\alpha$, we will reject the null hypothesis in favor of our alternate nypothesis, which is that the regression model does contain at least one useful predictor. That is what we want to achieve, because it says our model is useful on some level. Otherwise, we fail to reject our null hypothesis. We will be perform this hypothesis testing on each subplot.

$\alpha =0.05$

For wind:

$H_0:\beta _1=\beta _2=\beta _3=0$

$H_a:$ At least one $\beta _i\neq 0$ for $i=1,2,3$

The rest:

$H_0:\beta _1=0$

$H_a:\beta _1\neq 0$

Let's first start with the rest as their linear regressions make hypothesis testing much easier.

In [52]:
# get sources without 'wind'
linear_reg = np.delete(sources, np.where(sources == 'wind'))
linear_reg
Out[52]:
array(['bio', 'geo', 'hydro', 'nuclear', 'solar', 'wave', 'fossil'],
      dtype=object)
In [53]:
from statsmodels.regression import linear_model
from statsmodels.api import add_constant

for focus in linear_reg:
    # response
    Y = [get_total_quantity(i,focus)[0] for i in range(1990,2015)]
    # predictor
    X = add_constant([get_total_quantity(i,focus)[1] for i in range(1990,2015)])
    
    model = linear_model.OLS(Y, X)
    results = model.fit()
    print(focus)
    print(results.pvalues)
    print(results.summary())

bio

For each energy source, we print the pvalues and the summary for our fitted linear model. All the p-values are less than 0.05, so we reject the null hypothesis and say we have sufficient evidence to conclude that the one slope parameter of each source is not 0, so a linear relationship exists for all of them.

In [54]:
from statsmodels.regression import linear_model
from statsmodels.api import add_constant
from sklearn.preprocessing import PolynomialFeatures
import statsmodels.api as sm

focus='wind'
x=np.array([get_total_quantity(i,focus)[0] for i in range(1990,2015)])
y=np.array([get_total_quantity(i,focus)[1] for i in range(1990,2015)])

polynomial_features= PolynomialFeatures(degree=3)
xp = polynomial_features.fit_transform(x.reshape(-1,1))

model = sm.OLS(y, xp).fit()
ypred = model.predict(xp) 
plt.scatter(x,y)
plt.plot(x,ypred)
model.summary()
Out[54]:
OLS Regression Results
Dep. Variable: y R-squared: 0.951
Model: OLS Adj. R-squared: 0.946
Method: Least Squares F-statistic: 212.7
Date: Tue, 20 Jul 2021 Prob (F-statistic): 4.06e-15
Time: 03:00:59 Log-Likelihood: -296.40
No. Observations: 25 AIC: 598.8
Df Residuals: 22 BIC: 602.5
Df Model: 2
Covariance Type: nonrobust
coef std err t P>|t| [0.025 0.975]
const 5148.3279 470.033 10.953 0.000 4173.540 6123.116
x1 3.436e+06 3.14e+05 10.953 0.000 2.79e+06 4.09e+06
x2 -3440.8902 313.351 -10.981 0.000 -4090.741 -2791.040
x3 0.8615 0.078 11.009 0.000 0.699 1.024
Omnibus: 2.636 Durbin-Watson: 0.228
Prob(Omnibus): 0.268 Jarque-Bera (JB): 1.377

Kurtosis: 1.941 Cond. No. 2.21e+17


Notes:
[1] Standard Errors assume that the covariance matrix of the errors is correctly specified.
[2] The smallest eigenvalue is 3.31e-14. This might indicate that there are
strong multicollinearity problems or that the design matrix is singular.

For wind, we first have to tranform existing features into a cubic degree degree before we fit into our model. The p-values for all 3 of our coefficients are basically 0 so we reject the null hypothesis here too and say we have sufficient evidence to conclude that at least one of the slope parameters is not equal to 0.

The only issue here would be that the fitted cubic regression looks different from our polyfit cubic regression but this does not detract from the fact that a cubic regression is most fitting polynomial.

Final Question¶

Now that we have analyzed the data sufficiently to get a good idea of what is going on and we have the our regression equations, we will address our beginning question: will the total production of renewable and nuclear energy outcompete the total production of fossil fuels within the next 100 years(from 2014)?

To answer this, all we need is to plug in the year into our regression lines and do some simple arithmetic .

In [55]:
total=0
year=2114
for s in sources:
    if s=='wind':
        total+=objective(year, equations[s][0], equations[s][1], equations[s][2], equations[s][3])
    elif s=='fossil':
        total-=equations[s][0]*year+equations[s][1]
    else:
        total+=equations[s][0]*year+equations[s][1]
total
Out[55]:
-568712001.7048826

The total is negative, which means the predicted total production of wind, solar, geo, hydro, bio, and wave power was not able to beat the predicted total production of fossil fuels by 2114. We fail to reject the null hypothesis and sadly admit most of our produced energy still come from fossil fuels. However, that begs the question when is the earliest year that this does occurs?

In [56]:
for y in range(2114, 3114):
    total = 0
    for s in sources:
        if s=='wind':
            total+=objective(y, equations[s][0], equations[s][1], equations[s][2], equations[s][3])
        elif s=='fossil':
            total-=equations[s][0]*y+equations[s][1]
        else:
            total+=equations[s][0]*y+equations[s][1]
    if total>0:
        print('Year: '+str(y))
        print('Difference: '+str(total))
        break

Year: 2225
Difference: 6613490.443061113

Based on our current trends, we will break even with fossil fuels in 2225, 224 years from 2021! This may be too late for our planet though...

Conclusions¶

Although our result seems alarming, it should not be taken too seriously. The most recent year given is 2014 and we have certainly made a lot of progress since then. Additionally, the metric ton to kwh conversion with fossil fuels was heavily handwaved and many categories within might not have anything to do with producing energy. Finally, the further we predict into the future, the less accurate our models become. Of course the real amount of energy produced from fossil fuels will eventually stop increasing as by their nature as nonrenewable energy, we will run out at some point. However, these predictions still send the important message that our current rate of progress is too slow and if we want improvement in the near future, we must take drastic action to reduce our dependence on fossil fuels.
